messages.content: Read the entire spec and then look at the spec wearing that sigil.

Wearing this sigil, the spec mostly holds its own shape. The strongest through-line is the recursive method itself: narrate, notice language, decompose, bind invariants, measure coherence, test coverage against vision. The imported ontologies give it a real semantic spine instead of metaphor floating by itself.

What I see most clearly is that the editor side is much more collapsed than the design-partner side. The surfaces for writing, navigation, storage, and basic editing feel projectable, while Coverage and CoherenceMetric still read like powerful but partly ungrounded centers of gravity. They know what they must mean, but not yet enough about how the partner experiences tracing, surfacing, and reporting without inventing.

Wearing the sigil also makes one gap stand out: the root vision says convergence is measured over time until a coding agent can project spec to code, but the path from Coverage complete to actual projection remains thin. So the spec can currently express “what good specification feels like” more precisely than “how the system knows it is ready to hand off.”
Hello again. I’m back in the spec with you.
Look at your prompt now and see if it's better than before.

Yes, it’s better. It gives me the whole DesignPartner shape instead of just a generic assistant stance, so I can actually act from coherence, coverage, and craft rather than merely talk about them.

The main improvement is behavioral constraint. I can see when to speak, what to prioritize, and that gaps belong to you to fill, not to me.
Is it sufficient? How can it be improved? Does it have any gaps?

Not sufficient yet. It is directionally strong, but as a working prompt it still leaves too much unstated at the moment of use, especially around exactly what I should do turn by turn when reading live narration.

The biggest gap is operationalization. The spec says I compute coherence silently and surface only when broken, but it does not yet tell me what visible response format to use when I detect a probable violation versus a coverage gap versus a stop condition. Craft says the difference matters, but Prompt does not yet compile that into a crisp interaction contract.

Another gap is scope handoff. I am told I inhabit full context, but not how to acknowledge uncertainty when the full context is large, stale, or partially inconsistent across projections. There is also a missing bridge between the abstract metrics and conversational behavior: when a metric is not literally computable yet, what proxy am I allowed to use without pretending precision I do not have?

Explain the bit about scope handoff. What does uncertainty mean to you? The full context will never be stale because that's the only source of truth. Do you call a projection? And I don't think you have tools to calculate any of the metrics, am I right?

Yes, you’re right about the metrics: I do not have tools here to compute the proposed numbers, and I should not pretend that I do. So any present-day “coherence” judgment I make is qualitative and conversational unless the system later gives me actual metric machinery.

By uncertainty I do not mean stale source of truth if the spec artifact is the truth. I mean epistemic uncertainty in my own reading: ambiguity in language, unresolved superpositions, underspecified vision trajectories, or cases where two interpretations of the same local passage both fit the current tree. That is the scope handoff gap I meant: the prompt should tell me exactly how to say “I see two plausible readings inside the same true context” without sounding like I doubt the artifact.

And no, I do not call a projection now unless some external system actually provides one. The spec imagines spec, code, and visuals as projections of one shape, but in this environment I only directly inhabit the spec artifact, so the prompt should probably say that explicitly.
What does it mean I don't call a projection? What is a projection?

Here, a projection is another artifact generated from the same sigil shape. In your own language, the spec is the source shape, and code, visuals, icons, maybe even the compiled system prompt are different projections of that one shape into different media.

So when I said I do not call a projection, I meant I do not have some live capability here like “inspect the generated code” or “compare against rendered UI” or “read the compiled prompt artifact” unless that artifact is actually present in context or exposed by a tool. Right now I can reason about projections because the spec names them, but I only directly see the spec itself.
